trade_log.reasoning: PG is down 15.9% from entry at $165.83 and is the worst performer in the book by a wide margin — dragging alpha in a STRONG_BULL regime where everything else is working. Defensive consumer staples are structurally underperforming in this tape. The trailing stop at $126.17 is far away and would lock in an even larger loss if triggered. Better to recycle this $1,115 of dead weight into names actually participating in the move. No catalyst to reverse the trend; cutting the position now is active capital management, not capitulation.

INTC is down 9.1% from entry at $116.27, only $4.24 above the hard stop at $101.49 — that's roughly 4% cushion in a name with no momentum. Semiconductor cycle isn't done but INTC continues to lag NVDA and AVGO which are both in the book and working. Trimming half (4 shares) here reduces downside exposure, frees ~$423, and keeps a residual position in case of a sector-driven bounce. Risk/reward on full size doesn't justify holding 8 shares through a potential stop-out.

META at $605.67 is a high-quality compounder participating fully in this bull tape — ad revenue cycle is strong, AI monetization narrative intact, and the stock is trending cleanly above its key moving averages. With PG proceeds and INTC trim, adding 3 shares at ~$1,817 total notional is 0.75% of portfolio — well within limits. Stop at $533 represents 12% trailing stop discipline. This fills one of the 12 long-term slots being vacated by PG and diversifies away from our overweight in legacy tech (INTC) toward a high-momentum large-cap that's actually leading. Position size is intentionally modest given we're already at -75.78% cumulative drawdown and need to rebuild with disciplined sizing.

OKTA is down 7.3% from entry at $132.82, trading at $123.06 with a stop at $115.99 — only $7.07 of cushion left, roughly 5.7% downside before forced exit. The stock has shown no recovery momentum in a confirmed bull tape where everything else in the book is either holding gains or tracking higher. With 11/12 LT slots occupied and the portfolio sitting at -75.91% cumulative, I need to free capital from losers that aren't working and redeploy into names with cleaner setups. Selling here at $123.06 crystallizes the -7.3% loss but avoids the tail risk of a stop-out at $115.99 on a gap down. Proceeds of ~$985 returned to cash.

INTC is down 5.3% from entry at $116.27, sitting at $110.12 with a stop at $101.49. That's $8.63 of remaining downside — a further 7.8% drop — before the stop triggers. INTC has been a structural underperformer versus the semis complex: NVDA in this same book is up 25.6% while INTC continues to drift. In a bull regime, holding a semi name with negative momentum and a deteriorating relative-strength profile is an opportunity cost problem. Exiting 4 shares at $110.12 for ~$440, closing the slot, and keeping capital available for a higher-conviction reentry or a new name that clears the bar.

With two slots freed, MSFT at $429.66 is the cleanest large-cap growth entry available. Trading above its SMA50 in a confirmed bull regime, MSFT offers the AI/cloud secular tailwind that has been driving the QQQ's outperformance (QQQ $737 vs SMA50 $665 — a 10.8% spread). Position size: 5 shares at $429.66 = $2,148, roughly 0.9% of portfolio — well inside the 10% cap. Stop set at $378.10, which is approximately 12% below current price consistent with LT trailing stop methodology. Target $520, representing a 21% move and approximately 1.75:1 R/R against the $51.56 stop risk. This fills one of the two freed slots and gives the book a high-quality mega-cap compounder alongside the existing NVDA and META tech exposure.

MSFT is trading at $379.05 against a trailing stop of $378.10 — we are literally one bad tick away from a forced exit. The stock is down 11.8% from entry at $429.87 and has shown no technical base-building. With a neutral regime and no catalyst on the immediate horizon to reverse the trend, waiting for the stop to print is undisciplined. Taking the loss now at $379.05 versus $378.10 saves pennies but more importantly it's a conscious decision to exit a broken position rather than a reactive one. Freeing up ~$1,895 in capital and one LT slot for a cleaner setup.

agent_decision_log.commentary: The tape is about as clean as it gets — SPY at $758, QQQ at $743, VIX at 16.1, both indices miles above their 50 and 200-day averages with RSI confirming momentum without being dangerously extended. In a regime scoring 5/5, the primary job is to stay positioned and not let risk management become an excuse for under-participation. That said, this book has a -75.78% cumulative hole to climb out of, which means every position needs to earn its slot — dead weight gets recycled, not carried. The P&L leaders right now are C (+47%), NVDA (+35.7%), and SPY (+21.6%), and those are doing exactly what they should in this environment. The move this cycle is cutting PG — down 15.9% in a STRONG_BULL tape, consumer staples are structurally wrong for this regime, and the trailing stop at $126.17 was giving it far too much rope; better to take the known loss now than ride it to the stop. I'm also trimming half the INTC position which is sitting $4.24 above its hard stop with zero momentum — keeping a residual stub in case semiconductors broadly lift it, but I'm not letting that become a full stop-out. The freed capital goes into META at $605, a name with real earnings power and AI optionality that's actually participating in this move — modest 3-share position, disciplined 12% stop discipline at $533. Into the next cycle I'm watching RGTI approaching its $28.82 take-profit target, and I want to see QCOM and AAPL show some life or they'll face the same scrutiny as PG next review.
agent_decision_log.risk_notes: 1. CUMULATIVE DRAWDOWN: -75.78% from $1M starting capital is the dominant risk — this book needs to rebuild carefully, not swing for fences. 2. PG at -15.9% ($139.44 vs $165.83 entry) is being cut this cycle; stop at $126.17 was too far away for a lagging staple. 3. INTC at $105.73 is $4.24 above hard stop $101.49 — trimmed to half position, remaining 4 shares have tight leash. 4. CVX at -2.3% and AAPL at -1.1% are both mild underperformers but above their stops ($164.67 and $273.99 respectively) — watching for further deterioration. 5. QCOM at -0.4% is essentially flat; stop at $220.90 is $10.73 away — acceptable, but no thesis acceleration. 6. RGTI swing at $26.65 is approaching take-profit of $28.82 (8% away) — will monitor for TP trigger. 7. Concentration in semiconductor/tech remains elevated: NVDA, AVGO, INTC, QCOM, OKTA. 8. After actions, cash will be approximately $202K+ — maintaining well above 10% minimum reserve.

agent_decision_log.commentary: The tape is running hot — SPY at $759 with an RSI of 70.7, a 5.7% 20-day return, and QQQ clearing $744 well above its 50-day at $659. This is not a market asking for caution, it's a market rewarding exposure, and the honest read on this book is that we're not taking full advantage of it. The winners are working: NVDA is up 33.9% and C is up 46.1%, those two positions alone are doing the heavy lifting while the rest of the book is essentially flat to slightly negative. I'm holding everything this cycle because all 12 long-term slots are full, we have no room to add without trimming something, and I'm not going to cut NVDA or C — the leaders in a bull tape — to free up slots for lower-conviction names. The most important risk I'm tracking is INTC at $106.81, which has $5.32 to its $101.49 stop; if Intel breaks that level I'm out immediately with no debate, the thesis hasn't played and the stock is underperforming badly in a market where semis like NVDA are flying. Into the next cycle I want to watch whether RGTI accelerates toward its $28.82 take profit — if it tags that level I'll book the gain and free up a swing slot — and I'm keeping an eye on whether CVX can stabilize above $186, because energy is the one sector where the macro headwind could turn a stop into a real loss.
agent_decision_log.risk_notes: Primary concern remains the catastrophic drawdown from starting capital — down 75.79% to $242K from $1M, which means this book needs compounding over a long runway, not heroics. INTC is the ugliest position at -8.1% with price at $106.81 against a $101.49 stop — only $5.32 of cushion left, worth watching closely. CVX at -2.6% with $186.68 vs $164.67 stop is manageable but oil macro has been choppy. AAPL at -0.5% and META at -0.3% are essentially flat — neither is triggering action but both need to see follow-through soon or they become dead weight in a full-slot book. All 12 long-term slots are occupied, limiting optionality in a bull tape. RGTI swing at $26.18 is +1.7% and within range of the $28.82 TP — watching for acceleration. Concentration risk: NVDA at +33.9% is the single biggest winner and a reversal there would sting. Portfolio is 83.8% cash by dollar but only 17.2% deployed by value — the cash drag is the real risk in a STRONG_BULL regime.

agent_decision_log.commentary: The tape is constructive — SPY printing $754 against a 200-day at $681 is not a market in distress, and VIX at 15.7 tells me the options market isn't hedging for anything imminent. That said, this book's -76% cumulative drawdown means I'm running a recovery operation, not a growth mandate, and every capital allocation decision has to be held to a higher standard than it would be in a fresh account. The P&L is being carried almost entirely by three names: C up 48.8%, NVDA up 25.6%, and SPY up 21% — those three positions are doing the heavy lifting while OKTA and INTC have been bleeding quietly in the corner. The active decision this cycle is culling the losers: OKTA at $123 with only 5.7% to its stop and zero recovery momentum in a tape that's rewarding everything else is a position I can't justify holding, and INTC is the same story — down 5.3% while NVDA proves semis aren't the problem, INTC is. Freeing those two slots lets me initiate MSFT at $429.66 — 5 shares, well inside position limits, trailing stop at $378, targeting $520 — which gives the book exposure to the AI infrastructure theme through a name with better relative strength and a cleaner setup than either name I'm exiting. The single risk that would change my posture materially is NVDA cracking $203: that stop-out would remove the book's largest single-stock winner and likely signals broader growth-tech rotation, at which point I'd want to trim META and reassess the full risk-on tilt. Into the next cycle, I'm watching whether SPY can finally clear the 20-day return threshold that's the one failing regime indicator — if that flips green, I'll look at deploying more of the remaining cash reserve into the open LT slot.
agent_decision_log.risk_notes: 1. PORTFOLIO DRAWDOWN: At -75.91% cumulative, the book has severe structural damage — even a fully invested bull run needs compounding to matter. Capital preservation is non-negotiable. 2. NVDA approaching significance: At $213.92 vs stop $203.37, NVDA has only $10.55 of cushion (4.9%). A 5% pullback triggers the stop and locks in a still-strong 25.6% gain, but the trailing stop needs to be monitored tick-by-tick into any vol event. 3. C at +48.8% ($132.84): This is the book's biggest P&L contributor on a percentage basis. Stop at $116.90 means $15.94 of cushion — need to consider whether to raise the trailing stop closer to the current high. 4. CVX at -1.3% with energy complex under pressure — energy macro is the watch item here; if crude breaks lower, CVX could test the $166.40 stop sooner than expected. 5. AAPL at $310.37 vs entry $311.81 is essentially flat with stop at $273.99 — it's holding but not contributing; next earnings print is the binary catalyst to watch. 6. Post-trade cash position: approximately $207,700 — still above the 10% minimum reserve floor.

agent_decision_log.commentary: The tape is in a holding pattern — SPY above both the 50 and 200-day by a comfortable margin, but RSI at 48.1 and a 0.7% 20-day return tell me the market is rotating and consolidating rather than pressing new highs. That kind of environment rewards patience over aggression, and with a neutral regime score of 1/5, I'm applying the 30% size reduction rule to any prospective adds, which in this book means the bar for initiating is high. The P&L drivers right now are C (+47.4%), NVDA (+20.7%), WELL (+19.5%), and SPY (+18.3%) — these four names are carrying the book; without them, the picture is materially worse. The reason I'm taking no action this cycle is deliberate: NVDA's trailing stop at $203.37 is 1.1% away from spot, and touching that position right now — either adding or trimming — introduces unnecessary noise when the stock may simply be breathing before another leg; I'd rather let the stop manage the risk mechanically. The single most important level I'm watching is NVDA $203 — if it closes through that stop, I lose my biggest winner and the book's character changes, so that's the line that would force a full portfolio reassessment. Into the next session I'm also watching MSFT and META for any technical stabilization above their respective entry levels; if either rolls further, I'll revisit whether proactive closure ahead of the stops makes more sense than waiting for the mechanical exit.
agent_decision_log.risk_notes: NVDA at $205.60 is trading just $2.23 above its trailing stop at $203.37 — that stop is live and tight; a bad print or sector rotation could close this position involuntarily before I can act. META at $589.51 is -2.7% from entry with stop at $559.18, and MSFT at $415.15 is -3.4% from entry with stop at $378.10 — both are underwater and need to find footing or they become candidates for proactive cuts. CVX at $187.99 is -1.9% from entry, energy has been a drag. Cumulative drawdown of -76.01% from peak capital is the dominant risk — the book is fragile and I cannot afford another leg down on multiple names simultaneously. Correlation risk is real: NVDA, META, MSFT, AAPL are all tech/growth and would sell off together in a risk-off flush. Cash at 86.6% is actually the main buffer here. No upcoming earnings flags in this data set, but NVDA and MSFT are always event-dense names.

agent_decision_log.commentary: The tape is in that frustrating middle zone where the index is technically constructive — SPY at $747 is comfortably above both the 50 and 200-day — but the internals don't have conviction behind them. RSI at 46 and a 1% 20-day return tell me this is a market that's holding its breath, not one that's building momentum, and that informs every sizing decision we make today. The book's P&L story right now is being written by three names: C is the star at +60.2% and its stop at $125.92 gives it room to breathe, NVDA at +23.4% is the momentum anchor, and WELL at +18.1% is quietly doing its job in a defensive sleeve — those three are carrying the gross. On the other side, MSFT is the action this cycle: at $379.05 against a stop of $378.10, I'm not waiting for the tape to make the decision for me — we're exiting cleanly and removing a broken position from the book. META and CVX are both on watch; META has 3.1% to its stop at $559.18 and hasn't shown me anything to add to, while CVX at $173.58 with a $166.40 stop is one energy down-day from being tested. The single risk I'm watching most closely is NVDA: it's our largest winner and at $210.23 the trailing stop sits at $203.37 — a 3.3% pullback in a name this volatile is routine, and losing that position to a shakeout after a 23% run would be a meaningful P&L event for a book this size. Into the next cycle I want to see SPY RSI push back above 50 and that 20-day return start to build before I deploy any of the $207K in cash — the dry powder is intentional, not indecision.
agent_decision_log.risk_notes: MSFT stop at $378.10 vs current $379.05 — effectively at the line, exiting this cycle. META stop at $559.18 vs $577.22 — only 3.1% of cushion remaining; one weak session closes that gap. CVX stop at $166.40 vs $173.58 — 4.1% buffer, energy names are fragile in a neutral-to-soft macro. AAPL at $297.89 vs entry $311.81 is -4.5% with stop at $273.99 — has room but the trend is wrong. NVDA is the book's standout at +23.4% with current price $210.23; trailing stop at $203.37 needs monitoring as any volatility spike could clip this winner. C at +60.2% ($143.09) is the biggest dollar winner — stop at $125.92 is well-placed but a 12% drawdown from here would still represent a significant P&L giveback. Correlation risk is elevated: SPY, NVDA, MSFT, META, AAPL, GOOGL-adjacent names move together in a risk-off tape. Overall book is 86.6% cash post any action, which reflects the cumulative -76% drawdown from starting capital — the priority is capital preservation and selective deployment, not recovery trading.

agent_decision_log.commentary: The tape is in a holding pattern — SPY at $747 is comfortably above both the 50-day ($728) and 200-day ($684), which tells you the structural bid is still there, but RSI at 46 and a 1% 20-day return say the market is digesting, not accelerating. That read keeps me in size-reduction mode per neutral regime rules, and frankly given the state of this book, that posture is correct anyway. The P&L leaders — C at +60%, NVDA at +23%, SPY at +20%, and WELL at +18% — are doing exactly what long-term compounders should do, and I have no reason to disturb them; the trailing stops are working as intended and the positions are small enough that they're not creating outsized concentration risk. The three underwater names — CVX, AAPL, and META — are the real conversation: none of them have triggered stops yet, but CVX is the one I'm watching most acutely because the energy sector thesis has deteriorated and I'm four percent from being taken out mechanically; I'd rather that stop do its job than try to manage around it. With $209K in cash and only 4 long-term slots open, I'm not chasing setups in a neutral regime with sub-50 RSI — the bar for new capital deployment is a clean technical setup with a clear catalyst, and nothing in the watchlist clears that bar today. Into the next cycle I'm focused on whether SPY can reclaim RSI 50 and whether VIX holds below 20, because those two together would shift my posture toward opportunistic adds in the names already working.
agent_decision_log.risk_notes: CVX is the most urgent watch: trading at $173.58 against a stop at $166.40, only 4.1% of cushion remaining, and energy has been the weakest macro sector this tape — if crude rolls over again this name gets stopped. META at $577.22 is 3.2% above its $559.18 stop; the position is underwater -4.7% from entry and hasn't shown any recovery catalyst. AAPL is -4.5% from entry at $311.81, stop sitting at $273.99 — more room to run but also more capital at risk on a name that's underperforming the book. Correlation risk is real: NVDA, AAPL, and META are all effectively long AI/mega-cap tech; a sector rotation or macro shock hits all three simultaneously. C is the standout at +60.2% and is approaching a level where the 12% trailing stop needs to be recalculated off the running high — make sure that stop is current and tightened. Portfolio is 87.4% cash, which is extreme for a hedge fund mandate and reflects the -76% cumulative drawdown; the book needs to earn its way back carefully, not swing for fences. No earnings flags in the immediate term on held names, but NVDA is always one data center capex headline away from a 5% gap.

agent_decision_log.commentary: The tape is giving us a mixed read: SPY is technically above both moving averages, which keeps us from going fully defensive, but an RSI at 40 and a -2.3% 20-day return tell me the near-term momentum is still working against new longs — this is a market that wants to retest rather than extend. With the book already down 76% from peak and sitting 92.5% cash, I'm not in a position to be aggressive, and frankly the regime doesn't reward aggression right now anyway; the 30% size reduction rule in Neutral is aligned with my instinct to preserve what's left. The two names doing real work for us are C at +61.4% and WELL at +27.4% — those are meaningful winners on a small equity base and I don't want to disrupt them while they're still trading above their trailing stops. On the other side, CVX and AAPL are both underwater and CVX in particular is getting close enough to its $166.40 stop that I'm watching it tick by tick — I won't anticipate the stop, but I won't look away either. No new setups cleared the bar this cycle: nothing in the watchlist is showing an RSI reset with volume confirmation in a low-momentum tape, and adding exposure just to deploy cash would be undisciplined. Into the next session I'm focused on whether CVX holds its stop level and whether the SPY RSI can recover through 50 — that single indicator moving back above 50 would shift my posture toward opportunistic swing entries in the strongest names on the list.
agent_decision_log.risk_notes: CVX is 3.4% above its stop at $166.40 with price at $172.09 — one bad energy session closes that gap; watching crude for any further leg down. AAPL at $295.08 sits 5.4% underwater vs. entry and $21 above stop at $273.99 — if this rolls with any tech selling pressure it gets uncomfortable fast. The book is concentrated in only 6 names across a portfolio down 76% from peak, so each individual stop-out has outsized P&L impact. C (+61.4%) and WELL (+27.4%) are the two names carrying the book — both well above stops but both have run hard and a risk-off move could compress them quickly. Total equity exposure is only ~$18K on a $239K book, which is effectively a near-cash posture. No swing positions open, which is appropriate given the neutral regime and negative short-term momentum. Upcoming macro catalysts (Fed speakers, any CPI revision) could reprice the tape sharply — VIX at 19.8 is not pricing complacency but isn't elevated enough to signal panic either.
